downloadSentinel:
Core Validation Gate satisfied: Core Radiology content was retrieved from the uploaded Core PDF under **Vascular Imaging → Traumatic aortic injury, pages 547–549**, with additional IR treatment mention on **IR page 604**.

Whole-source coverage audit completed from full Core-retrieved content and the full RadPrimer article text.
Core Summary was incorporated into the shared Anki summary field; card generation used Core + RadPrimer synthesis, not the digest alone.

Total cards to export: 33
Expected TSV lines: 33

Download: [radprimer_cards.tsv](sandbox:/mnt/data/radprimer_cards.tsv)

CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_BEGIN
CORE_EVIDENCE_STATUS: USED
CORE_SOURCE_BASIS: Core Radiology: A Visual Approach to Diagnostic Imaging, 2nd edition; uploaded Core PDF. Vascular Imaging chapter, Traumatic aortic injury section, visible pages Vascular 547–549; additional Interventional Radiology trauma/aortic injury coverage visible on IR page 604.
CORE_FACTS_USED:

* CTA is the gold standard / modality of choice for suspected traumatic aortic injury.
* Classic injury locations are relatively fixed aortic levels, especially the aortic isthmus; also root and hiatus.
* Direct CT signs include intimal irregularity/flap, pseudoaneurysm, dissection, and intramural hematoma.
* Mediastinal hemorrhage separated from an intact aorta by a fat plane can be presumed venous; hemorrhage contacting the aortic wall suggests aortic injury.
* Minimal aortic injury is approximately 10–30% of blunt traumatic aortic injuries in Core and involves only the intima.
* Mechanisms include rapid deceleration, shearing forces, water-hammer phenomenon, and osseous pinch.
* Chronic post-traumatic pseudoaneurysm may show peripheral calcification and thrombus.
* Open surgical repair is described in Core/IR as gold standard historically, with endovascular stent-graft repair increasingly performed.
CORE_DERIVED_CARDS:
* UNKNOWN reasoning for acute traumatic aortic injury cases, especially isthmus localization and direct CTA sign interpretation.
* Differential Drill distinguishing true aortic injury from branch-vessel infundibulum / ductus diverticulum / venous mediastinal hemorrhage.
* Mechanism card: “Why is the aortic isthmus the classic site of traumatic aortic injury?”
* Mechanism card: “Why does a traumatic aortic pseudoaneurysm represent contained rupture rather than a stable lesion?”
* Boards Trap card about venous mediastinal hemorrhage versus aortic wall injury.
* High-Yield cards covering CTA as modality of choice, minimal aortic injury frequency, and chronic pseudoaneurysm imaging.
CORE_LIMITATIONS:
* Core did not provide the RadPrimer-specific CTA sensitivity/specificity values, radiographic sign percentages, SVS grading treatment details, BP targets, TEVAR-over-open recommendations, or untreated mortality figures; those were taken from the provided RadPrimer article only.
CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_END

RADPRIMER_CARD_TSV_DOWNLOAD_READY
FILENAME: radprimer_cards.tsv
EXPECTED_ROWS: 33
